lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16: Vision and Language — Multi-Modal Foundation Models

unit.name: Advantages and limitations of CLIP-style models
unit.description: Surveys what makes CLIP useful in practice and where CLIP-style contrastive training still fails.
unit.summary: The lecturer identifies three major advantages of CLIP-style models: the dot-product similarity computation is simple and efficient, embedded datasets enable fast retrieval, and the model is open-vocabulary because users can provide arbitrary text descriptions [ts=1215s] [ts=1227s] [ts=1247s]. CLIP is also easy to chain with other systems, a theme returned to later in the lecture [ts=1261s]. The limitations begin with compositional failures such as confusing “a mug in grass” with “grass in a mug,” showing that CLIP may not represent relationships between concepts reliably [ts=1273s] [ts=1388s]. The lecturer explains that contrastive learning depends heavily on batch size: larger batches are more likely to contain hard negatives that force fine-grained discrimination, but even very large batches do not guarantee robust representations [ts=1293s] [ts=1327s] [ts=1357s]. Handcrafting hard negatives can backfire by hurting semantic generalization [ts=1426s] [ts=1452s]. Finally, image-level captions lack grounding: they do not tell the model where the person, car, or street is, and even billions of images may not cover all important visual concepts [ts=1497s] [ts=1521s].

key_points.text: CLIP-style dot-product similarity is efficient for both training and inference.
Embedding a dataset once enables fast retrieval over CLIP representations.
Open-vocabulary use lets users query with arbitrary text descriptions.
CLIP can fail at compositional distinctions such as “mug in grass” versus “grass in a mug.”
Large batch sizes help because they increase the chance of hard negative examples.
Image-level captions omit grounding information such as object locations and relationships.

kp_catalog.name: CLIP retrieval, open-vocabulary use, and chainability
kp_catalog.description: CLIP is useful because its dot-product similarity enables fast retrieval, arbitrary text queries, and composition with other models.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cLIP retrieval, open-vocabulary use, and chainability clarifies cLIP is useful because its dot-product similarity enables fast retrieval, arbitrary text queries, and composition with other models, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: CLIP limitations: batch size, compositionality, grounding, and coverage
kp_catalog.description: CLIP struggles with relational compositionality, depends on large/hard-negative batches, lacks localization grounding, and cannot cover all concepts through web data alone.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because cLIP limitations: batch size, compositionality, grounding, and coverage clarifies cLIP struggles with relational compositionality, depends on large/hard-negative batches, lacks localization grounding, and cannot cover all concepts through web data alone, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
